coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin C3N4 nanosheets-based oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme for dual-mode detection of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Yizhong
Gao, Xiang
Chen, Huanhuan
Wei, Yunlong
Yang, Hui
Gu, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Engineering efficient dual-mode portable sensor with built-in cross reference correction is of great significance for onsite reliable and precise detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) and evading the false-positive outputs, especially in emergency case. Currently, most nanozyme-based sensors for OPs monitoring primarily replied on the peroxidase-like activity, which involved unstable and toxic H2O2. In this scenario, a hybrid oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme (PtPdNPs@g-C3N4) was yielded by in situ growing PtPdNPs in the ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet. When acetylcholinesterase (AChE) hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to thiocholine (TCh), it ablated O2 −• from the dissolved O2 catalyzed by PtPdNPs@g-C3N4’s oxidase-like activity, hampering the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). Consequently, with the increasing concentration of OPs which inhibited the blocking effect by inactivating AChE, the produced DAP caused an apparent color change and a dual-color ratiometric fluorescence change in the response system. Through integrating into a smartphone, a H2O2-free 2D nanozyme-based onsite colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode visual imaging sensor for OPs was proposed with acceptable results in real samples, which holds vast promise for further development of commercial point-of-care testing platform in early warning and controlling of OPs pollution for safeguarding environmental health and food safety.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin C3N4 nanosheets-based oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme for dual-mode detection of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Engineering efficient dual-mode portable sensor with built-in cross reference correction is of great significance for onsite reliable and precise detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) and evading the false-positive outputs, especially in emergency case. Currently, most nanozyme-based sensors for OPs monitoring primarily replied on the peroxidase-like activity, which involved unstable and toxic H2O2. In this scenario, a hybrid oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme (PtPdNPs@g-C3N4) was yielded by in situ growing PtPdNPs in the ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet. When acetylcholinesterase (AChE) hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine (ATCh) to thiocholine (TCh), it ablated O2 −• from the dissolved O2 catalyzed by PtPdNPs@g-C3N4’s oxidase-like activity, hampering the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). Consequently, with the increasing concentration of OPs which inhibited the blocking effect by inactivating AChE, the produced DAP caused an apparent color change and a dual-color ratiometric fluorescence change in the response system. Through integrating into a smartphone, a H2O2-free 2D nanozyme-based onsite colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode visual imaging sensor for OPs was proposed with acceptable results in real samples, which holds vast promise for further development of commercial point-of-care testing platform in early warning and controlling of OPs pollution for safeguarding environmental health and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the misuse in agriculture, the pesticide residues in environmental substrates have been recognized as one of the most serious threats to environmental health and food safety [38,40,46,54]. Thereinto, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are a class of the small molecular phosphate ester compounds that exhibit an excellent broad-spectrum insecticidal activity, enabling them to be the most popularly substances against insects in improving the economic interests of the agricultural production processes [17,43,47]. However, in contrast to the economic benefits, the misuse of OPs will unavoidably pose a certain residues in crops (e.g., teas, vegetables, & fruits), soil, and surface water, resulting in a potential exposure risk for public health [10,13,16]. It has been proved that the long-term exposure to OPs, i.e., even at extremely low level, may also cause severe side effects including immune dysfunctions, nerve injuries, and poisoning-triggered death in some severe cases [18,33,36,48]. In this situation, the official agencies around the world have set the maximum allowable residual threshold of OPs in all kinds of foods. For instance, the National Standards of the People’s Republic of China [37] and the U.S. Department of Agriculture set the residual threshold of OPs in food matrices at 0.01 ppm [39]. On account of this, it is of great significance to develop an efficient onsite analytical method for warning-early of OPs residues in environmental matrices.

Recently, artificial nanozymes have attracted great interests in exploiting them to explore efficient optical assays for monitoring OPs residues, because of the lower cost and more robust enzyme mimic activity than that of natural enzymes [2,22,23,30,9]. In principle, these nanozymes-based analytical techniques for OPs residues primarily reply on the residual OPs-modulated reactive oxygen species (ROS, e.g., •OH) release/inhibition for further triggering the optical signal changes [25,52,56,57]. Among them, the peroxidase-like nanozymes are the most common for developing the optical assays toward OPs residues, in which usually employ H2O2 as a co-reactant. However, it has been discovered that H2O2 suffers the troublesome drawbacks of the high toxicity and poor stability, limiting the practical application of the peroxidase-like nanozymes-based assays in onsite detection of OPs residues [44]. Under this background, the H2O2-free-involved oxidase-like nanozymes-based optical assays toward OPs residues have become research hot topic. For example, Wang and co-workers reported an ingenious colorimetric paper sensor for precise detection of OPs residues by utilizing oxidase-mimicking γ-MnOOH nanowires [12]. Chen et al. employed the Fe-N5 active center-loaded hierarchically porous carbon nanoframes as an oxidase-like single-atom nanozyme to propose another efficient colorimetric assay for monitoring the residual levels of OPs [4]. Different from the single analytical mode in these examples above, Niu group recently proposed a ratiometric fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode analytical platform for detection of OPs residues by applying oxidase-mimicking trifunctional Ce,Tb co-coordinated polymers [20]. It is well-known that dual-mode analytical strategy effectively established a built-in cross reference correction capacity to reduce/avoid the effects of test environments, resulting in a higher accuracy for OPs than that of single analytical mode [5,19]. As expected, this oxidase-like activity-based dual-mode assay has exhibited an acceptable result for precise and reliable detection of the residual OPs. Even so, it has to rely on the experimental bulky apparatus, failing to the demands of the onsite analysis toward OPs residues, particularly in emergency case. To the best of our knowledge, there was a very rare concern on developing the H2O2-free-involved oxidase-like nanozymes-based dual-mode onsite assays toward OPs residues so far.

Owing to the large surface to volume ratio, strong photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, and low cost, ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheet has been employed as an excellent substrate to anchor various nanoparticles with in situ growth way, which provided a feasibility to design efficient g-C3N4-based 2D multimodal analytical platforms [49,51,6]. Enlightened by these works above, we here engineered a novel oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme based on the PtPdNPs-decorated g-C3N4 nanosheets (PtPdNPs@g-C3N4) by growing in situ PtPdNPs on g-C3N4 nanosheets, which was further matched with non-fluorescent OPD to establish a H2O2-free-involved oxidase-like nanozyme-based colorimetric-ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode onsite assay for OPs residues in safeguarding food safety ( Fig. 1). As the efficient oxidase-like activity from the in situ growing PtPdNPs, PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 triggered non-fluorescent OPD to emit yellow fluorescence at ∼ 550 nm (as response signal), attributable to the production of fluorophore DAP with naked-eye dark-yellow, which matched with the constant blue fluorescence emission of g-C3N4 inside PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 at 446 nm (as reference signal) for employing as a typical dual-color ratiometric fluorescence readouts. At this moment, the naked-eye chromogenic reaction from light-yellow to dark-yellow was observed. However, both the naked-eye chromogenic reaction and dual-color ratiometric fluorescence response were remarkably hampered by exploiting AChE to catalyze ATChC for generating TCh. As such, the reaction system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 only emitted at blue channel fluorescence and exhibited a naked-eye light-yellow. Considering the inactivation behavior of OPs toward AChE, OPs could prevent TCh generation to re-activate the oxidase-like activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for oxidizing OPD into DAP through a competitive effect, recovering the naked-eye chromogenic reaction and dual-color ratiometric fluorescence response. By integrating with a smartphone-based sensor, an onsite visual colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging platform was proposed for reliable and precise reporting the OPs residues in food matrices, which was employed trichlorfon (Trich, a member of OPs) as a proof-of-concept for successful verification. Compared with previous reports on the use of fluorescence for the detection of OPs [14,15,20,24,34], the nanozyme PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 could act stronger oxidase-like activity, leading to the more sensitivity of analysis of the OPs. And the dule-mode sensing attributed to the excellent oxidase-like activity and fluorescence characteristic could get more detection accuracy and reduce the likelihood of false positives. This work offered a novel insight in utilizing oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme to develop efficiently multimodal detection and assessment platform in residual OPs-triggered environmental health and food safety risks.

All reagents and chemicals used in this work were acquired from commercial suppliers and were used as received without further pretreated. Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6), acetylthiocholine chloride (ATChC), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and permethrin were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Palladium acetate (Pd(CH3COO)2) were purchased from Haohong scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trichlorfon (Trich) and dihydroethidium (HE) were obtained by Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Superoxide anion kit was supplied by Solarbio Science and Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). MgCl2, CuSO4, NaHCO3, NaCl, Na2CO3, NaNO3, NaH2PO4, glucose, glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys), and glutathione (Gsh) were purchased from Sinopharm Holding Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Flubendiamide, thiamethoxam and dinotefuran were purchased from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). HAc-NaAc buffer was bought from Phygene Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE), glutamic acid were supplied by Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Corporation (St Louis, MO, USA). Singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) was acquired from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Deionized (D.I.) water (Millipore, USA, 18.2 MΩ resistivity) was used in all experiments.

The fluorescence data were acquired on a HITACHI F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Company, Japan), and the absorption data were obtained on a UV–vis TU-1901 spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). The TEM images was gathered on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan). Zeta potential and hydrodynamic sizes of nanoparticles were collected on a Zetasizer (Nano-Z, Malvern, UK). The XPS analysis was carried out on the ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). A Bruker EMX-10/12 X-band variotherm was applied to collect the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. The pH values were adjusted by a PHS-3 C pH meter (Leici, Shanghai, China). The XRD analysis was performed on a MiniFlex 600 (XRD, Rigaku, Japan). The AFM data were collected from Dimension Icon (AFM, BRUKER, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme PtPdNPs@g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: According to the prievious report, the crude C3N4 powder was firstly prepared by calcining melamine (5.0 g) in a muffle furnace for 2.0 h at 600.0 ℃ [53]. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained C3N4 powder received 12.0 h ultrasound treatment in D.I. water, which was further peformed the freeze drying. After that, the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheet with blue fluorescence emission was prepared. To synthesize PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, 8.2 mg of H2PtCl2, 4.0 mg of Pd(CH3COO)2, and 5.0 mg of g-C3N4 were dissolved into 7.0 mL D.I. water for receiving 10.0 min ultrasound treatment, which was then placed on a magnetic stirrer for 1.0 h. At this point, 1.1 mg of NaBH4 was added into the above solution, whose color was changed from light yellow to black. After another 2.0 h stirring, the precipitate was collected by 12000 rpm centrifugation for 3.0 min in a high-speed centrifuge, which was washed with anhydrous ethanol and D.I. water, respectively, and then dried in an oven at 70.0 °C. Finally, the PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 powder was stored and reserved to perform further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity of 2D fluorescence nanozyme PtPdNPs@g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para:
1.0 mM of TMB was mixed with 60.0 μg/mL of g-C3N4, 60.0 μg/mL of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, and 60.0 μg/mL of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 + N2 in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 5.0, 5.0 mM), respectively. After complete mixing, these samples were placed in a metal bath at 35.0 °C for 15.0 min, and their absorbance at 650 nm were measured. In addition to that, the apparent steady-state kinetic experiments of the PtPdNPs@g-C3N4-catalyzed TMB oxidation were identified by variation of TMB concentration in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH=5.0, 5.0 mM) containing 60.0 μg/mL PtPdNPs@g-C3N4. As a result, the Michaelis constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max) were determined by the following Michaelis-Menten equations (Eq. 1): [30]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode spectral response of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 toward Trich and visual imaging of Trich on smartphone-based portable sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The 20.0 mU/mL of AChE was incubated with different concentration of Trich (0.0–200.0 ng/mL) at 35.0 ℃ for 15.0 min, respectively. After that, the 10.0 mM of ATChC was added into the solution to incubating for at 35.0 °C for 10.0 min. At this point, the 60.0 μg/mL of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 and 10.0 mM of OPD in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5, 5.0 mM) was mixed into the above solution to incubating at 35.0 °C or another 20.0 min (The incubation time all were the optimal). After that, the absorption spectra in colorimetric mode were recorded with the UV–visible spectrophotometer and the fluorescence emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm in ratiometric fluorescence mode were recorded with the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

In HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5, 5.0 mM), the same experimental operation were done as in the spectral detection. For the implementation colorimetric and fluorescence imaging of Trich on this smartphone-based portable sensor, 80.0 μL solution from the above mixture after completing the reaction was dropped onto the filter paper. The colorimetric images of Trich at different concentrations on this smartphone-based sensor were obtained at a optical power of 125.0 μW/cm2 and a whistling height of 20.0 cm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence images of Trich at different concentrations on this smartphone-based sensor were obtained by exciting the filter paper with a 365 nm UV lamp of 3.0 W/cm2 and a whistling height of 30.0 cm. To investigate the selectivity of this sensor toward Trich, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, PO4 3-, H2PO4 -, CO3 2-, Cl-, Br-, I-, flubendiamide, thiamethoxam, dinotefuran, permethrin, tea polyphenol, glutamic acid, cysteine, glutathione, glucose, dimethoate, glyphosate, phosphine acetyloxalamine, parathion-methyl, Trich (all the sample were 100.0 ng/mL) were done with the same experimental operation described before. After that, 80.0 μL solution from the above mixture after completing the reaction was dropped onto the filter paper for obtaining the colorimetric and fluorescence images. All the images obtained from the smartphone-based sensor were converted to RGB chroma values by the installed Color Picker APP for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Onsite visual dual-mode imaging of Trich in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 g of each sample (i.e., green tea, red tea, apple, orange, and cabbage, were purchased from a local market in Hefei, Anhui, China) was shredded and immersed in 10.0 mL of D.I. water for 30.0 min, and then the supernatant was retained by centrifugation to remove the precipitate for the next step [50]. In brief, the five supernatant solutions from different kinds of samples were mixed with different concentration of Trich (1.0 ng/mL, 10.0 ng/mL, 50.0 ng/mL), and then these spiked samples were incubated with 20.0 mU/mL of AChE at 35.0 °C for 15.0 min, respectively. After that, the 10.0 mM of ATChC was added into the solution to incubating for at 35.0 °C for 10.0 min. At this point, the 60.0 μg/mL of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 and 10.0 mM of OPD in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5, 5.0 mM) was mixed into the above solution to incubating at 35.0 °C for another 20.0 min. Then the 80.0 μL of the solution from the above mixture was dropped onto the filter paper for obtaining the colorimetric and fluorescence images, and converted to digital RGB values by the installed Color Picker APP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of oxidase-like 2D fluorescence PtPdNPs@g-C3N4-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the 2D fluorescence nanozyme PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheet with thickness below 1.40 nm was firstly prepared by employing as the scaffolding for growing in situ PtPdNPs (Fig. S1, S2, & S3). As depicted in Fig. 2 A, we intuitively observed the dispersive nanoparticles on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheet. As described in Fig. 2B to H, the presence of C, N, O, Pt, and Pd elements in the EDX and elemental mapping images suggested the successful growth of PtPdNPs on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheet, which agreed well with the subsequent XPS analysis (Fig. S4 & S5). The high-resolution Pd 3d binding energy spectrum exhibited four peaks at 335.48, 337.78, 340.73 and 342.88 eV, respectively. Among them, the peaks at 335.48 and 340.73 eV were primarily contributed to the metallic Pd(0) state of Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, respectively, and the other peaks at 337.78 and 342.88 eV were resulting from the Pd(II) oxidation state [8]. Meanwhile, the high-resolution Pt 4 f binding energy spectrum displayed two main peaks at 71.34 and 74.53 eV, attributable to the metallic Pt(0) state of Pt 4 f5/2 and Pt 4 f7/2 [26]. By comparing the high-resolution N 1 s binding energy spectra between along g-C3N4 and PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, their characteristic peaks of tertiary N (-N(C)3) were almost identical, while the characteristic peak resulting from the sp2-hybridized N (C-N = C) in alone g-C3N4 was 0.10 eV smaller than that in PtPdNPs@g-C3N4. These results were probably ascribable to the chelation of the metal ions with the sp2-hybridized N (C-N = C) in g-C3N4 to in situ grow the PtPdNPs for obtaining the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 [32]. To confirm this deduction, both the FT-IR and UV–vis absorption analyses were performed. As presented in Fig. 2I, it was clear that the FT-IR absorption band among 3000–3500 cm−1 in PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 showed the higher frequency than that in alone g-C3N4, revealing the production of Pd-N or Pt-N bonds in the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 [42,51]. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 J, the characteristic UV–vis absorption peak of alone g-C3N4 at 320 nm was red-shifted to 330 nm in that of the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, and the UV–vis absorption band among 400–800 nm inside alone g-C3N4 was much smaller than that inside the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, further confirming the occurrence of metal-ligand covalent behavior [58]. To further prove the above arguments, we also performed XRD characterization analysis. As shown in Fig. S6, there are a same peak at 27.38° both in g-C3N4 and PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 which was due to the (002) plane of C3N4. And the PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 provided some new peaks at 40.00°, 46.58°, and 67.88° were allocated to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of the face-centered cubic structure of Pt and Pd (JCPDS 04-0802, JCPDS 46-1043) [26,51]. Apart from these, as depicted in Fig. 2 K, the surface zeta potential of the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 (−8.4 ± 1.2 mV) was more positive than that of alone g-C3N4 (−23.1 ± 1.8 mV), probably attributable to the positive charge metal ions in the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4. Based on these findings, the metal-ligand covalent behavior played an important role in the in situ growing of the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4. Notably, the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission at 446 nm upon different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 L) [29]. Moreover, by incubating the hybrid PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 in aqueous solution over half of month, there was insignificant fluorescence change, revealing a nice stability (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity of 2D fluorescence PtPdNPs@g-C3N4-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the oxidase-like activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, TMB was employed as the substrate to perform colorimetric assay by exploiting the absorbance of its oxidative product ox-TMB at 650 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 A & 3B, only the mixture of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 with TMB displayed a conspicuous chromogenic reaction, together with a remarkably enhanced absorbance at 650 nm. In contrast, the N2-pretreated mixture of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 with TMB showed a weaker chromogenic reaction as well as lower absorbance at 650 nm. These facts implied a strong oxidase-like catalytic activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for catalyzing the dissolved O2 into radicals. Meanwhile, the mixture of g-C3N4 with TMB exhibited negligible chromogenic reaction upon the same experimental conditions, verifying that the oxidase-like catalytic activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 was primary resulting from the in situ grown PtPdNPs. To explore the origin of the oxidase-mimicking feature of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments were carried out to measure the generating radicals by employing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as radical scavenger. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 C, an obvious superoxide radical (O2 −•) signal was captured. Meanwhile, dihydroethidium (HE, a special O2 −• fluorescence probe) showed a significant fluorescence enhancement before and after incubation with PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 (Fig. S8) [11,55]. These results clearly implied the generation of O2 −• by PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to catalyse the dissolved O2, which was further proved by the absorbance enhancement of O2 −• kit at 542 nm (Fig. S9) [7]. Moreover, there was no singlet oxygen (1O2) peak in the EPR of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 by employing 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as an 1O2 trap (Fig. 3D), and there was an insignificant fluorescence change of singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG, a 1O2 fluorescence indicator), clearly ruling out 1O2 generation caused by PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to catalyse the dissolved O2 (Fig. S10) [31]. According to these facts above, it could be concluded that PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 was capable of catalyzing the dissolved O2 to O2 −•, endowing PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to exhibit oxidase-like activity to oxidize TMB into ox-TMB. On account of this, the maximal reaction velocity (V max) as well as Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 were acquired from the Michaelis-Menten equation-plotted Lineweaver-Burk curves (Fig. S11 & Table S1). As compared with other reported oxidase-like nanozymes, the V max and K m of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 were comparable or even lower than these catalysts, indicating that PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 possessed a prominent oxidase-like activity. Noticeably, the optimal oxidase-like activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 appeared at pH = 5.0 and 35.0 ºC, because the maximal absorbance after the chromogenic reaction at 650 nm corresponded to 35.0 ºC (Fig. 3E) and pH = 5.0 (Fig. 3 F), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode spectral response of Trich using PtPdNPs@g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that OPD could be oxidized into DAP by ROS, emitting yellow fluorescence signals and resulting in a naked-eye chromogenic reaction-triggered absorbance enhancement at 435 nm (Fig. S12) [21]. Given such unique property, we envisioned to construct a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-mode signal output unit (OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4) by exploiting OPD to match with PtPdNPs@g-C3N4. To obtain the optimal response parameters, the colorimetric assay was adopted to optimize pH, AChE and ATChC amounts. The results exhibited that the maximal absorbance of the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 in response to Trich appeared at pH = 5.5 (Fig. S13), 20.0 mU/mL AChE (Fig. S14), and 10.0 mM ATChC (Fig. S15), respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A & 4B, PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 initially emitted a blue channel fluorescence signal at 446 nm, which kept constant after incubating with OPD, companied by a new yellow channel fluorescence signal at 550 nm. As such, there was an obvious naked-eye chromogenic reaction from light-yellow to dark-yellow, along with a remarkable absorbance enhancement at 435 nm. These findings confirmed that the oxidase-like activity of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to convert the dissolved O2 into O2 −•, leading to the oxidation of OPD into DAP. However, both the naked-eye chromogenic reaction and yellow channel fluorescence emission were significantly blocked by the mixture of AChE with ATChC, revealing that the hydrolysate TCh, from AChE toward ATChC, and OPD reacted competitively with the O2 −• and weakening the production of DAP. Interestingly, the above competitive phenomena were ineffective after utilizing Trich to pre-treated AChE, because that Trich could inactive AChE and not hydrolyze ATChC to produce the reductive TCh, making O2 −• work to oxidize OPD into DAP. Inspired by these facts, the effects of Trich concentration among 0.0–200.0 ng/mL on the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the system AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 in aqueous solution. As presented in Fig. 4 C & D, there was a clear Trich concentration-dependent naked-eye chromogenic reaction from light-yellow to dark-yellow, along with a remarkable absorbance enhancement at 435 nm, thereby proposing a linear equation of A = 0.00975 [Trich] + 0.31328 (R 2 = 0.9982). Correspondingly, as shown in Fig. 4E & F, there was an unambiguous Trich concentration-dependent yellow channel fluorescence enhancement, along with an almost constant blue channel fluorescence, observing a gradually increase of bright-yellow fluorescence color, thereby yielding a typical ratiometric response via the intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 446 nm versus Trich concentration. By plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 446 nm versus Trich concentration, a linear relationship of F 550 nm/F 446 nm = 0.01171 [Trich] + 0.52457 (R 2 = 0.9991) could be proposed. These results indicated a feasibility by employed the oxidase-like activity of the 2D fluorescence nanozyme PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to develop a visual onsite colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensor toward OPs. In order to demonstrating the assay stability of the material, we compared the detection effect of the system AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for Trich at 0.0 ng/mL and 100.0 ng/mL with the PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 which have stored at room temperature for more than thirty days and the newly synthesized PtPdNPs@g-C3N4, and found that there was no significant difference in the assay results between the two, demonstrating the long-term stability of our material (Fig. S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual dual-mode imaging of Trich on smartphone-based portable sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we focused on developing a portable sensor based on this system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 to achieve the onsite visual dual-mode imaging of Trich residues. Taking into account the photography and image data processing capabilities of the popular smartphone, a smart workshop was design to assemble miniature 365 UV lamp, smartphone, ﬁlter paper strip, mobile power, and a long-pass filter (Fig. S17). As exhibited in Fig. 5 A, without launching the mobile power, the built-in camera could directly capture a series of naked-eye photographs with a successive change from light-yellow to dark-yellow with the Trich concentration among 0.0–100.00 ng/mL. After initiating the mobile power to light-up UV lamp for exciting the ﬁlter paper strip, the built-in camera gathered a progressively bright yellow fluorescent images with the Trich concentration among 0.0–100.0 ng/mL as well. By converting these images into digital RGB parameters (i.e., blue (B) green (G) & red (R)) through the Color Picker APP, a linear relationship expressed as G/B = 0.01090 [Trich] + 1.09865 (R 2 = 0.9963) in naked-eye colorimetric mode was proposed with a linear range among 0.28–50.0 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 0.083 ng/mL (3σ/slope, σ suggested the standard deviation from the replicate measurements of 11 blank samples, Fig. 5B). Similarly, a linear equation of G/B = 0.02824 [Trich] + 1.09739 (R 2 = 0.9925) in fluorescence mode was also discovered with a linear range among 0.11–50.00 ng/mL and a low detection limit of 0.033 ng/mL (Fig. 5 C). As compared with other reported techniques for Trich and other kinds of OPs residues ( Table 1), the detection limit in this portable sensor based on the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 responding to Trich was comparable and even lower, which was even lower than that of the maximum allowable residue of 0.1–0.5 ng/mL for the National Standards of the People’s Republic of China [37] and 0.01 ppm (10.0 ng/mL) for the U.S. Department of Agriculture [39]. These findings exhibited the high sensitivity of this dual-mode portable sensor based on the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for Trich residues detection.

To estimate the selectivity of this dual-mode sensor based on the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for Trich, some representative contents were employed as the proof-of-concept to perform the onsite visual colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging, including Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, PO4 3-, H2PO4 -, CO3 2-, Cl-, Br-, I-, flubendiamide, thiamethoxam, dinotefuran, permethrin, tea polyphenol, glutamic acid, cysteine, glutathione, glucose, dimethoate, glyphosate, phosphine acetyloxalamine, parathion-methyl, and Trich, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 6 A & 6B, these indicated contents could cause insignificant naked-eye and fluorescence color changes on this smartphone-based sensor. Comparatively, all the tested OPs (i.e., dimethoate, glyphosate, phosphine acetyloxalamine, parathion-methyl and Trich) had the similar ability to trigger the obvious color changes both in the naked-eye and fluorescence dual-mode. In the view of these results, it could be speculated that this this dual-mode sensor based on the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 possessed a high selectivity and an impressive anti-interference capability for onsite visual colorimetric and fluorescence imaging of the residual total OPs in a reliable and accurate way, which might be suitable for carrying out the onsite traceability analysis of the total OPs residues in complicated food matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Onsite visual dual-mode imaging of Trich in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: After revealing the satisfactory response sensitivity and selectivity of this colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging sensor based on the system of AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 for reporting the levels of Trich, its practical value for Trich monitoring in actual food matrics was ultimately examined by the believable standard addition method. As manifested in Table 2, the relative standard deviations (R.S.D.) ranging from 0.73 % to 3.27 % demonstrated a prominent repeatability of this dual-mode imaging sensor in response to onsite monitor the residual Trich in actual food matrices. Additionally, the recoveries among 95.78–104.34 % implied a high accuracy of this dual-mode imaging sensor in response to onsite monitor the residual Trich in actual food matrices. The actual samples which were purchased from the market that were not spiked were tested, and found that the OPs residues were extremely low and exceeded our detection limits. Based on these results, it could be assured that such colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging sensor had a nice practicability for the accurate and reliable oniste visual monitoring of the residual Trich in complicated real samples.

In summary, an efficient H2O2-free AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4-integrated smartphone-based colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging sensor had been proposed to actually and reliably report the residual levels of OPs in complicated food matrices with onsite visual way. In this design, the oxidase-like activity of 2D fluorescence PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 enabled it to catalyze the dissolved O2 into O2 −•, which could effectively oxidize the substrate OPD into DAP, leading to an obvious naked-eye chromogenic reaction. Meanwhile, by employing the intrinsic blue channel fluorescence of PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 as reference signal and the yellow channel fluorescence of the produced DAP as response signal, a typical dual-color ratiometric fluorescence change was observed as well. Nevertheless, the hydrolysate TCh from AChE toward ATChC was capable of scavenging the O2 −•, blocking both the naked-eye chromogenic reaction and dual-color ratiometric fluorescence change. Benefitting from the inactivation behavior of OPs toward AChE, the residual OPs possessed the ability to prevent the hydrolysate TCh production, restoring both the naked-eye chromogenic reaction and dual-color ratiometric fluorescence change through a competitive effect. As such, there were OPs concentration-dependent colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode spectral response. On this basis, the AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 was integrated into smartphone for developing a colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode imaging sensor, realizing the accurate and reliable onsite visual monitoring of OPs residues in real food matrices with acceptable results. Based on these findings, we believed the great prospects by designing g-C3N4 nanosheet-based oxidase-like 2D fluorescence nanozyme to combine with smartphone for establishing efficient dual-mode point-of-care imaging sensors in ensuring the table security and environmental health. Although this work has shown the great advantages of the AChE-ATChC-OPD-PtPdNPs@g-C3N4 integrated with smartphone, the use of AChE will lead to some shortcoming due to the high cost and low stability of AChE for the future commercialization of the sensor.

As one of the most threatening risk factors for environmental health, organophosphorus pesticide (OPs) residues in environment-related matrices have evoked worldwide attention, but how to establish a proper management mechanism for its pollution still remains many challenges. To date, the reported peroxidase-like nanozymes-based assays mainly involved harmful H2O2. For this, we here designed an oxidase-like 2D fluorescent nanozyme-driven colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode portable sensor for onsite visual detection of OPs without H2O2. This work holds vast promise for further development of commercial point-of-care testing platform in early warning and controlling of OPs pollution for safeguarding environmental health and food safety.

Yizhong Shen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiang Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft.
